full_text: Hi guys, my name is Nitesh and you are welcome to my YouTube channel. In this video also we will continue our Lang chain playlist. Before starting the video, I would like to give you a quick recap of what we have covered in this playlist so far. I have put 15 videos in this playlist and we can divide these 15 videos into two parts. In the first part, we have covered the fundamentals of the Lang chain. In the second part, we have covered the components of the Lang chain. We discussed the components one by one like the components of models, prompts, chains etc. In the second part, we used the Lang chain to learn how to make rag systems. We have covered topics like document loaders, text splitters, vector stores, retrievers and finally we learned how to make rag based systems. Now, from today's video, we are going to start the third part of this playlist. We will learn how to build agents using the Lang chain. In this part, I am going to add 3-4 videos and today's video will be the first video for this segment. In today's video, we are going to learn about the tools in the Lang chain. I will explain in detail the concept of tools, how it fits in the agent roadmap and how to create different types of tools in the Lang chain. On the whole, if you want to learn how to make agents using the Lang chain or Lang graph, you should have a good knowledge about tools. In that sense, today's video is super important. So please make sure you watch this video end to end. Now, let's start the video. Before moving ahead in the video, I want to give you an overview of the topics we are going to cover in this segment. First of all, we will cover tools, which is the topic of today's video. Here we will learn to work with different types of tools and learn to make our own tools. After that, we will cover a concept in the next video, which is called tool calling. So what you do in tool calling is that you connect your LLM with each other so that they can work together. So this is the topic of our next video. After that, we will cover the concept of agents. Here we will use the Lang chain to make agents. You will notice that in the process of making these agents, you will use the concept of LLMs, tools, and tool calling. So basically, whatever you have studied so far, everything will be connected and we will cover all that in the agent. So I may need a couple of videos on the topic of agents. But the idea is that I want to explain in detail how agents are made using the Lang chain. So now that you have this high-level overview of what we are going to do in the next two or four videos, now let's start today's video and focus on tools. So let's talk about tools. But to understand the tools, we will have to talk about LLMs first. So LLMs, as you know, are very powerful natural language processing systems. If I ask you what is the biggest quality of LLMs, what is the biggest power of LLMs, then you will probably say two things. First, you will say that LLMs have reasoning capability, which means that if you provide a question to LLMs, then LLM understands that question and breaks down how to answer that question. So basically, the LLM can think. Right? This is the first core capability of an LLM. The second one is language generation. Once LLMs understand how to answer a given question, then LLMs generate answers word by word. Which means in a way we can say that LLMs have the capability to speak. So in a nutshell, today's LLMs have two core capabilities. First, to think. Second, to speak. But that's it. Apart from this, LLMs have no other power. For example, if I ask LLMs what is the best way to go to Mumbai from Delhi, then they will think and tell you the answer. That flight is one option, train is one option, bus is another option. But if I tell them to book my train, can LLMs book it for you? The answer is no. LLMs don't have the power to perform tasks for you. So in a way you can say that LLMs are like a human body that has the capability to think and speak, but that human body doesn't have hands or legs. Which means that it can't execute tasks on its own. So there are a lot of tasks that computer systems can do today, but LLMs can't. For example, fetching live weather data. LLMs can't. Reliability of maths. LLMs can perform basic addition and subtraction. But if you give it a complex maths problem, there is a good chance that the answer won't be reliable. Because LLMs have not learned how to solve maths. They have learned language generation. LLMs can't call external APIs. If you say that tweet on Twitter on my behalf, then LLMs can't. They can't run code and can't interact with databases. So in the real world, computer systems that can perform a lot of tasks, LLMs don't have that capability. So as I said, you can see LLMs like a human body that has the capability to think and speak, but that human body doesn't have hands or legs. So what are tools? Tools are a mechanism through which you can give LLMs hands or legs. Meaning, you can create a tool to perform any given task and connect that tool with your LLMs. As soon as you perform this connection, as soon as that task is given to LLMs, then LLMs will execute that task with the help of that tool. Technically speaking, tools are nothing but functions. So this is the logic of executing a task. Now what you do is you package this function in such a way that it can interact with LLMs. So for example, you created a function that can perform booking on IRCTC's website. So now you can see that LLMs are able to communicate with the LLMs. So now as soon as I ask LLMs to tell me about the trains from Delhi to Mumbai, then LLMs will tell me. Now as soon as I ask LLMs to book a train for me, then since LLMs have access to this function, then LLMs will book a ticket for you with the help of this function. And that is the power of tools. The more tools you add to LLMs, the more tasks your LLMs can perform. Here a very simple definition is written. A tool is just a Python function that is packaged in a way that LLMs can understand and call when needed. So LLMs will think about when and what tools they need. And then they will call that tool and provide inputs. That tool will execute its task and will tell LLMs about it. LLMs will tell you that the task is done. And this is the flow. So I hope you have roughly understood the concept of tools. Tools is a way to provide LLMs hands and legs. So that they can execute any kind of tasks. Now if we discuss about tools a little more, then you get two types of tools in Lankchain. One is built-in tools and the second is custom tools. So Lankchain's team identified that there are many tasks that everyone needs. For example, searching on Google, internet, Wikipedia, command line tools. So they have already made built-in tools for these popular use cases and kept them in Lankchain. Anyone can use these tools and you don't need to write any code yourself. And the second is custom tools. It is possible that you are making a system for your company with LLM plus tools. And you get a use case that is only specific to your company. So what you can do there is that you can make custom tools for yourself. So going forward, I will teach you to work with both types of tools. I will show you the built-in tools in Lankchain. I will also show you how to make your own tools and how to connect them with LLM. Now a big question is how the concept of tools is related to the concept of agent. Because after all we want to learn how to make agents. So let's read the definition of agent here. See what is written here. So agent is a system that has two capabilities. First, it can think, reason and second, it can take action. Meaning it can perform tasks about the things that it has thought. So in a nutshell, agents have two capabilities. One is reasoning and decision making. Given a problem, they can think step by step about how to solve that problem. And once they think, they can actually perform action. Now this reasoning and decision making part of agent comes from LLM. But this action taking part that I have to do now, you power it using tools. So in a nutshell, the marriage of LLM and tools is called agent. And that is why the important concept of LLM is as important as the concept of tools. And that is why I told you in the beginning of the video that if you want to make agents with the help of LLM and LLM, then tools is a concept that you should know well. So I really hope that you understood the discussion we had in the last 5 minutes. And now we will try to understand how to make tools and how to use them. So first I will teach you how to work with built-in tools. By the way, we just discussed what are built-in tools. A built-in tool is a tool that LLM already provides for you. It's pre-built, production ready and requires minimal or no setup. So you don't have to write the function logic yourself, you just have to import and use it. The basic idea is that there were some popular use cases that LLM team felt that everyone will need it. So they have already made those tools and put them in the library of LLM. Now if you want to use those tools, then you don't have to write your own code. Simply import them and use them. So here I have written the name of some popular built-in tools and their one-line description. For example, one tool is DuckDuckGo Search. With the help of this you can do web search. DuckDuckGo is a very famous search engine like Google. So to go to DuckDuckGo and search, a tool is already made in the LLM. Second is Wikipedia Query Run. You can go and search on Wikipedia and get a summarized version of it with the help of this tool. After that comes Python Ripple Tool. With the help of this you can run raw Python code. Suppose you want a factorial of some number. If you directly tell LLM, then it's not necessary that the answer you get will be correct. Instead, you can use this tool. This tool will write a code of factorial behind the scenes and will give you a flip-up answer. After that is Shell Tool. With the help of this you can run shell commands or run commands in the console. So you are making an application where you want to interact with the files of the program on your system. So you can use this tool there. Other than that is Request Get Tool. With the help of this you can request HTTP. Gmail Send Message Tool. With the help of this you can email using Gmail. There is a different tool for Slack. SQL Database Query Tool is also there with the help of which you can run SQL queries. So you can see that the popular use cases have tools already made in the LLM. So what I will do is I will show you how to work with one or two tools. It's very simple but once you see it, you will understand and develop it. So what I will do is I will take you to Google Colab where I have already written this code. You just have to import some things. And this is our first tool that we are going to see. We will use DuckDuckGo Search. So it is possible that you are making an agentic application where you need to search the web in real time. Suppose your user comes and asks you what is the most important news today. chunks.text: Hi guys, my name is Nitesh and you are welcome to my YouTube channel.

So LLMs, as you know, are very powerful natural language processing systems.
If I ask you what is the biggest quality of LLMs, what is the biggest power of LLMs, then you will probably say two things.
First, you will say that LLMs have reasoning capability,
which means that if you provide a question to LLMs, then LLM understands that question and breaks down how to answer that question.
So basically, the LLM can think.
Right? This is the first core capability of an LLM.
The second one is language generation.
Once LLMs understand how to answer a given question, then LLMs generate answers word by word.
Which means in a way we can say that LLMs have the capability to speak.
So in a nutshell, today's LLMs have two core capabilities.
First, to think. Second, to speak.
But that's it.
Apart from this, LLMs have no other power.
For example, if I ask LLMs what is the best way to go to Mumbai from Delhi,
then they will think and tell you the answer.
That flight is one option, train is one option, bus is another option.
But if I tell them to book my train, can LLMs book it for you?
The answer is no. LLMs don't have the power to perform tasks for you.
So in a way you can say that LLMs are like a human body that has the capability to think and speak,
but that human body doesn't have hands or legs.
Which means that it can't execute tasks on its own.
So there are a lot of tasks that computer systems can do today, but LLMs can't.
For example, fetching live weather data. LLMs can't.
Reliability of maths. LLMs can perform basic addition and subtraction.
But if you give it a complex maths problem, there is a good chance that the answer won't be reliable.
Because LLMs have not learned how to solve maths. They have learned language generation.
LLMs can't call external APIs.
If you say that tweet on Twitter on my behalf, then LLMs can't.
They can't run code and can't interact with databases.
So in the real world, computer systems that can perform a lot of tasks, LLMs don't have that capability.
So as I said, you can see LLMs like a human body that has the capability to think and speak,
but that human body doesn't have hands or legs.
So what are tools? Tools are a mechanism through which you can give LLMs hands or legs.
Meaning, you can create a tool to perform any given task and connect that tool with your LLMs.
As soon as you perform this connection, as soon as that task is given to LLMs,
then LLMs will execute that task with the help of that tool.
Technically speaking, tools are nothing but functions.
So this is the logic of executing a task.
Now what you do is you package this function in such a way that it can interact with LLMs.
So for example, you created a function that can perform booking on IRCTC's website.
So now you can see that LLMs are able to communicate with the LLMs.
So now as soon as I ask LLMs to tell me about the trains from Delhi to Mumbai,
then LLMs will tell me.
Now as soon as I ask LLMs to book a train for me,
then since LLMs have access to this function,
then LLMs will book a ticket for you with the help of this function.
And that is the power of tools.
The more tools you add to LLMs, the more tasks your LLMs can perform.
Here a very simple definition is written.
